summary: Dan Luu challenges the common startup pitch that equity compensation has higher expected value than cash packages from large tech companies. He argues that if startup options were truly as valuable as claimed, companies could sell more equity to VCs and pay employees in cash instead. Through detailed analysis of how employee stock options differ from investor preferred stock (liquidation preferences, dilution, exercise windows), tax implications, and historical data showing VC funds don't outperform the market, he demonstrates that most startup equity packages are worth significantly less than advertised. The piece systematically dismantles both cynical and non-cynical justifications for option-heavy compensation, concluding that the arrangement primarily benefits founders and investors at employees' expense.
key_points: Employee common stock options are fundamentally different from VC preferred stock due to liquidation preferences, anti-dilution clauses, and dilution effects
VC funds don't outperform other investment classes on average, suggesting startup equity doesn't carry a justified risk premium
Most startup options are significantly overvalued when presented to candidates through misleading metrics like strike price or last valuation
Common justifications like retention and incentive alignment are contradicted by evidence from cash-paying companies like Netflix
Over 50% of options are returned to the pool when employees leave, making them effectively free or heavily discounted for companies
money_quotes: If you wanted to get rich, how would you do it? I think your best bet would be to start or join a startup. That's been a reliable way to get rich for hundreds of years" and "Risk and reward are always proportionate." This risk-reward assertion is used to back the claim that people can make more money, in expectation, by joining startups and taking risky equity packages than they can by taking jobs that pay cash or cash plus public equity. However, the premise — that risk and reward are always proportionate — isn't true in the general case.

If you look at companies that have made a lot of people rich, like Microsoft, Google, Apple, and Facebook, almost none of the employees who became rich had an instrumental role in the company's success.

As an employee who gets startup options, you don't (directly) pay VC fees, which means you can beat the market by keeping the VC fees for yourself. Another argument is that, some investors (like YC) seem to consistently do pretty well. If you join a startup that's funded by a savvy investors, you too can do pretty well. For this to make sense, you have to realize that the company is worth more than "expected" while the company doesn't have the same realization because you need the company to give you an option package without properly accounting for its value.

At rates big companies are paying today (mid-band of perhaps $380k/yr for senior engineer, $600k/yr for staff engineer), working at a big company and spending $25k/yr investing in startups is strictly superior to working at a startup from the standpoint of financial return.

Maybe so, but that's not a counter-argument — it's an argument that the most startups don't have options that are valuable enough to be exchanged for the equivalent sum of money, i.e., that the options simply aren't as valuable as claimed.
key_insight: The persistent use of stock options over cash compensation in startups is better explained by information asymmetry and the ability to obscure true compensation value than by any legitimate financial or incentive-alignment benefit to employees.
